answers.value:
The first indication that there might be an issue with this project arose internally rather than through a formal complaint. On 2024-10-02, our Senior Structural Engineer, Sarah Thompson, sent an internal email to me (Michael Reeves, Managing Director) and to our Project Director, David Ng, noting that the as-built survey drawings for the Seaview Apartments carpark did not fully align with the final set of structural drawings we had issued in June 2023. She highlighted that the actual slab thickness and reinforcement spacing in certain portions of the basement carpark appeared to deviate from our design intent, based on construction photos and site notes she had recently reviewed while preparing marketing material for a separate tender. 

Following that email, on 2024-10-04, David Ng and I had a meeting with our Quality Assurance Manager, Linda Park, to review the design files, RFIs, and site inspection reports. At that time, we noted a potential exposure but no known damage or complaint from the client, Seaview Developments (2021) Limited, or from the body corporate that was in the process of being formed. We agreed to keep the matter under review and to obtain updated information from the contractor’s as-built documentation once it was formally issued. 

On 2024-10-10, our site inspection engineer, Jack Wilson, reported verbally to David Ng that during an informal conversation with the main contractor’s site manager, the site manager had mentioned minor cracking observed in some of the basement carpark slabs approximately six months after completion. This was not documented as a defect at the time because the contractor believed it to be shrinkage cracking only. Jack’s notes from that conversation were added to our internal project file on 2024-10-12.

Therefore, prior to receiving the formal letter of complaint on 2024-11-18, the following people within Harbourview Consulting Engineers Limited had some level of awareness of a potential issue:
- Michael Reeves (Managing Director) – aware from 2024-10-02 via Sarah’s email and subsequent QA meeting.
- David Ng (Project Director) – aware from 2024-10-02 via the same email and 2024-10-04 QA meeting, and further informed by Jack Wilson on 2024-10-10.
- Sarah Thompson (Senior Structural Engineer) – first to identify the possible misalignment between as-built conditions and design intent on 2024-10-02.
- Linda Park (QA Manager) – became aware during the QA review meeting on 2024-10-04.
- Jack Wilson (Site Inspection Engineer) – had knowledge of anecdotal reports of cracking from the contractor as of 2024-10-10.

At that stage, none of us had received any formal allegation of negligence or defect from the client, contractor, or any third party. Our awareness was limited to a potential discrepancy and unconfirmed reports of cracking, which we were in the process of investigating further when the formal intimation of claim arrived on 2024-11-18.

answers.field_label: Details Of Allegation If No Proceedings
answers.value:
On 2024-11-18, we received a formal letter of complaint and intimation of claim from the solicitors acting for Seaview Developments (2021) Limited, the developer of the Seaview Apartments complex located at 14-22 Marine Parade, Petone, Wellington. The letter was sent by Oceanview Legal Limited and addressed to Harbourview Consulting Engineers Limited for the attention of the Managing Director, Michael Reeves. 

The letter alleges that:
1. Harbourview Consulting Engineers Limited, in its capacity as structural engineer for the Seaview Apartments project, failed to exercise reasonable skill and care in the design of the post-tensioned concrete slabs forming the basement carpark and ground-floor podium.
2. As a result of this alleged failure, excessive deflection and cracking have developed in various portions of the basement carpark slab and in parts of the podium slab supporting landscaped areas and a communal terrace.
3. The alleged defects have purportedly led to:
   - Ponding of water in the basement carpark, causing nuisance to residents and increased maintenance costs.
   - Cracking of applied floor finishes and waterproofing membranes on the podium, leading to localised water ingress into two ground-floor apartments and the basement-level storage rooms.
   - A reduction in the market value of the affected units due to perceived structural concerns and ongoing remedial works.

The letter encloses a preliminary report prepared by an independent engineering consultancy, Coastal Structures & Materials Limited, dated 2024-10-29. That report states, in summary, that:
- Visual inspections identified multiple instances of flexural cracking in the basement slab exceeding what the author considers typical shrinkage cracking.
- Measured slab deflections in certain carpark bays were said to be greater than predicted by standard design assumptions for the given spans.
- In at least one location, measured cover to reinforcement was less than that shown on our drawings, and reinforcement spacing appeared to be inconsistent with the design documentation.
- While the report acknowledges potential construction-related issues, it concludes that there is at least a prima facie basis to question the adequacy of both the slab thickness and reinforcement detailing specified in our structural drawings.

The complainant’s solicitors assert that, based on this report and associated repair estimates obtained from two contractors, the developer has suffered or will suffer loss and damage in the estimated amount of NZD 485,000 (inclusive of investigation costs, temporary relocation of affected residents, and provisional remedial works). They state that this figure is preliminary and may be subject to revision once a full remedial scope is confirmed.

The letter further alleges that we:
- Failed to adequately consider long-term deflection under sustained load, including the effect of additional imposed loads from planters and water features on the podium.
- Did not carry out sufficient site inspections or respond adequately to RFIs concerning reinforcement placement during construction.
- Signed off on practical completion without properly verifying that the as-built slab thickness and reinforcement matched the design drawings and calculations.

The correspondence to date consists of:
- The initial intimation letter dated 2024-11-18 from Oceanview Legal Limited.
- An enclosed copy of the Coastal Structures & Materials Limited preliminary report dated 2024-10-29.
- A follow-up email dated 2024-11-25 from Oceanview Legal Limited, attaching additional photographs of cracking and ponding, and requesting that we confirm within 20 working days whether our professional indemnity insurer has been notified and whether we will participate in a joint investigation.

The allegations are currently at the stage of a formal claim letter and supporting expert report only. No formal court proceedings, adjudication, or arbitration have been commenced as at the date of completing this form. However, the tone of the correspondence makes it clear that, if the matter cannot be resolved by agreement, the developer intends to pursue legal action against our firm to recover the claimed losses.

answers.value:
Harbourview Consulting Engineers Limited takes these allegations very seriously. We have commenced a detailed internal technical review of the project, including re-checking the original design calculations, reviewing all construction-phase correspondence and site reports, and comparing the issued-for-construction drawings with the available as-built information.

By way of context, our role on the Seaview Apartments project was as structural engineer engaged by the developer under a standard NZS 3910-based consultancy agreement. Our scope included design of the primary structure, preparation of structural drawings and specifications, limited site observation (monthly inspections and specific hold-point inspections), and review of the contractor’s shop drawings. We did not have a construction management or clerk-of-works role, and day-to-day supervision of construction remained with the main contractor.

Our preliminary findings to date are as follows:

1. Design adequacy:
   - The original design of the basement and podium slabs was carried out in 2021 by our then Senior Structural Engineer, Peter Lawson, and checked by Sarah Thompson. The design was based on the relevant New Zealand Standards (including NZS 3101 for concrete structures) and took into account the specified live loads for carparks and landscaped podium areas.
   - A re-run of the key span and deflection checks using our current software (with updated load combinations) indicates that, assuming the slabs and reinforcement were constructed in accordance with our drawings, the design is conservative or within acceptable limits. Long-term deflection was assessed and control joints were detailed to manage shrinkage cracking.
   - At this stage, we have not identified an obvious error in the underlying design calculations that would, on its own, explain the extent of cracking and ponding alleged.

2. Construction and as-built discrepancies:
   - The limited as-built information provided to us so far, together with construction photographs, suggests that there were deviations from our design in at least some areas. These apparent deviations include reduced slab thickness at certain edges and deviations in reinforcement spacing and cover.
   - Our site inspection reports from the relevant period show that we raised concerns about reinforcement congestion and cover in several locations, and that we requested corrective measures. In each case, the reports were signed by the contractor’s site manager as having been actioned. We did not, however, carry out full-time observation of all slab pours.
   - It is therefore possible that at least part of the observed cracking and ponding is attributable to construction-related issues, including workmanship, curing practices, and any unapproved changes made on site.

3. Extent and nature of damage:
   - From the photographs and the independent engineer’s preliminary report, the cracking observed appears to be a combination of expected shrinkage cracking and some areas of wider flexural cracking. Without our own detailed on-site investigation (including measurements of crack widths, deflections, and concrete properties), we cannot yet agree with the independent engineer’s assessment of severity.
   - Ponding in a carpark environment, while undesirable, does not necessarily indicate a structural safety issue. Rather, it may be more a serviceability and user-amenity concern. We note that the report does not state that the structure is unsafe or non-compliant with ultimate limit state requirements.

4. Causation and responsibility:
   - At this stage, we consider that the cause of the reported issues is likely multifactorial. Potential contributing factors may include: deviations from the design during construction, changes in loading (e.g., heavier landscaping elements than originally allowed for), and environmental or maintenance factors.
   - We do not accept, at this preliminary stage, that the issues can be attributed solely or primarily to negligence on the part of Harbourview Consulting Engineers Limited. That said, we acknowledge that we owe a duty of care to the developer and subsequent owners, and we are committed to engaging constructively to identify the root cause and, if appropriate, contribute to a pragmatic remedial solution.

5. Our response and proposed next steps:
   - We have acknowledged receipt of the developer’s letter through their solicitors and advised them that this matter has been notified to our professional indemnity insurer in accordance with our policy obligations.
   - We have proposed a joint investigation involving our firm, the developer’s independent engineer, and, if necessary, a mutually agreed third-party expert to undertake detailed measurements and testing. This would include non-destructive testing of slab thickness, reinforcement location, and concrete strength, as well as a detailed survey of slab levels and ponding.
   - We have requested full access to all construction records, including concrete delivery dockets, site diaries, variation orders, and any instructions that may have altered slab depths or reinforcement during construction.

6. Reservation of rights:
   - While we wish to cooperate fully and in good faith, we have made it clear in our correspondence that we reserve all our contractual and legal rights and defences. Until a thorough technical investigation has been completed, we cannot admit liability for the claimed losses or agree to the quantum of NZD 485,000 (or any higher figure that may later be proposed).

In summary, our current position is that:
- We are actively investigating the allegations and are prepared to participate in a collaborative technical review.
- We have not identified, at this stage, a clear and unequivocal design error on our part that would fully account for the alleged damage.
- There is preliminary evidence of construction deviations and other potential contributing factors that must be properly considered before any liability can be determined.

We will continue to gather and review all relevant information and will keep both the claimant and our insurer informed as the investigation progresses. We are open to mediation or other alternative dispute resolution processes once the technical facts are more clearly established.
